content.text: Hook handlers are the executable units that Claude Code spawns at lifecycle checkpoints. Writing an effective hook means authoring a script or server that reads a structured JSON event payload, makes a decision, and communicates that decision back via exit code and a JSON response on stdout — all within a well-defined contract that Claude Code enforces at runtime.
A hook is composed of two parts: a
that maps an event name (and optional matcher) to a handler, and a
— a shell script, HTTP server, Claude prompt, or subagent — that implements the logic. The binding controls
the hook fires; the handler controls
The most common handler type is

, which is any executable file or shell expression. Claude Code invokes it as a subprocess, writes the event payload as JSON to its stdin, waits for it to exit, and then reads two signals: the exit code (0, 1, or 2) and any JSON written to stdout. The combination of those two signals determines what Claude Code does next — continue, log a warning, or block the current action.

Without hooks, Claude Code's session lifecycle is opaque. You can steer Claude through prompts and permissions, but you cannot intercept individual tool calls, inject external context at prompt submission time, or automatically react when Claude finishes a turn. Hooks break open that black box.
Consider a team working on a production codebase. Without hooks: a developer asks Claude to clean up a build directory and accidentally includes a destructive

hook: that command is intercepted, denied, and the reason is shown to Claude — all before a single byte is deleted. The same hook infrastructure enables auto-formatting after every file write, CI integration, prompt context injection, and real-time logging — patterns that would otherwise require custom tooling or CI wrappers.
: one hook script blocks a destructive command for every session, every developer, on every project — no CLAUDE.md reminder needed.
: a PostToolUse formatter runs exactly when a file is written, not on a polling interval.
: every tool call and its result can be streamed to a structured log, metrics system, or audit trail without changing Claude's behavior.
: a UserPromptSubmit hook can add the current git diff, the linked Jira ticket, or the project's coding standards to every Claude prompt automatically.
: asyncRewake hooks run CI or tests in the background and wake Claude only when they fail, keeping the session interactive while ensuring correctness.
: hooks run as your OS user. A hook that writes files or calls external APIs has exactly your access level — no sandboxing, no privilege reduction.
: blocking hooks on latency-sensitive events (UserPromptSubmit has a 30 s ceiling; MessageDisplay has 10 s) directly delay the UI. A slow network call inside a synchronous hook is felt by the user.
: a hook that exits with code 1 is logged but does not interrupt Claude. Bugs in synchronous enforcement hooks may silently allow actions you intended to block.
No security boundary for the if filter
field is a performance optimization — it can fail open when commands cannot be parsed, so it must not be relied upon as a security control.
When a lifecycle event fires, Claude Code evaluates all hook groups registered for that event. Each group has a
field that filters by tool name, agent type, or session start reason — depending on the event. Inside a matching group, individual handlers can further narrow with an
field. When a handler passes all filters, Claude Code spawns it (for
type) and sends the event JSON payload.
For command handlers, the payload arrives on
as a UTF-8 JSON object. The handler reads it, performs its logic, then terminates. Two outputs matter: its
and any JSON object it writes to
. The JSON object must have a top-level
key whose value is an event-specific sub-object. Claude Code ignores any output that does not match the expected shape.
The three exit codes cover the full decision space:
means the hook ran cleanly and either made no decision (let the normal permission flow apply) or wrote a decision JSON that Claude Code should apply.
is a soft error — the hook failed, Claude Code logs it, and execution continues.
is a hard signal: for PreToolUse, it denies the tool call; for hooks with
, it wakes Claude and feeds the hook's stderr back as a system message.
The following diagram traces the full execution path from a hook configured in
through routing and filtering to the handler script and finally to the decision Claude Code applies:

attrs.alt: Hook execution flow: settings.json → routing/filtering → handler script → decision

content.text: Each column maps to a distinct phase: configuration (what events and matchers), routing (which groups pass the filter), execution (what the handler does with stdin), and decision (how Claude Code interprets exit code and stdout).
Use a hook when you need logic that the permissions system cannot express — logic that depends on the

of a tool call, not just its name. The permissions system decides allow/deny by tool name and static rules; a hook can inspect the command string, validate a file path, call an external service, or consult a policy database.
Blocking specific shell patterns (e.g., any command that modifies files outside the project directory)
Running a formatter, linter, or type-checker after every file write
Appending git diff, current branch name, or linked ticket details to every prompt
Streaming every tool call to a structured audit log or metrics pipeline
Running a test suite in the background and waking Claude when tests fail (asyncRewake)
Allowing or denying a tool by name alone — use the permissions system, which is evaluated before hooks and is more efficient
Enforcing organizational policy across many teams — use managed policy settings, which cannot be overridden by user hooks
Complex stateful multi-step orchestration — that belongs in the prompt or a subagent, not a hook
Step 1 — Choose the Event and Handler Type
Identify which event fires at the moment you want to intercept. For blocking logic,
is the primary choice — it fires before the tool runs, giving the hook the chance to deny. For post-processing, use
. For background validation, use any tool event with
A minimal command hook in bash reads stdin, extracts the relevant fields, applies logic, optionally writes a decision JSON to stdout, and exits with the appropriate code. The handler
before attempting to call any other command — stdin is a one-shot stream and is consumed by the first reader.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# 1. Drain stdin FIRST — it can only be read once.
payload=$(cat)

# 2. Extract fields using jq (or python3 -c / node -e)
tool_name=$(echo "$payload" | jq -r '.tool_name // empty')
command=$(echo "$payload"  | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')

# 3. Apply logic
if echo "$command" | grep -qE 'rm[[:space:]]+-[rf][rf]?[[:space:]]'; then
  # 4a. Write a denial decision to stdout
  printf '%s\n' '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"Recursive rm blocked by project policy"}}'
  # 5. Signal a hard block
  exit 2
fi

# No decision — normal permission flow applies
exit 0
Step 3 — Register the Binding in settings.json
Step 4 — Test with a Mock Payload
Before relying on a hook in a real session, test it directly by piping a representative payload into the script and inspecting the exit code and stdout output:
Every hook receives a JSON object on stdin. The base fields are always present; additional fields depend on the event type.
The event type string, e.g. PreToolUse, Stop, UserPromptSubmit
Stable for the duration of one Claude Code session
The tool being called, e.g. Bash, Edit, mcp__memory__store
The arguments passed to the tool; shape varies by tool
The result the tool returned; useful for logging or validation
alternative) to safely extract optional fields — this produces an empty string rather than the string
when a field is absent, which makes downstream
The JSON a hook writes to stdout must wrap its event-specific payload under
. The inner object's required fields differ by event.
to block the tool call, or omit it entirely (along with exit 0) to defer to the normal permission flow. The
is shown to Claude so it can adapt its next action.
string is appended to Claude's system prompt for this turn only, so it sees the injected information as part of its context without the user having to type it.
, Claude Code re-enters the agentic loop after the hook exits. This enables "always check tests after stopping" patterns: the hook runs tests, and if they fail it returns
to tell Claude to keep going.
HTTP hooks POST the event JSON to a URL you specify. The server must respond with HTTP 200 and an optional JSON body in the same
shape. This makes hooks composable with any existing HTTP-capable service — a compliance gateway, an audit logger, or an approval workflow.
A minimal Python server that enforces a blocklist of shell prefixes:

from http.server import BaseHTTPRequestHandler, HTTPServer
import json

BLOCKED = ["curl ", "wget ", "ssh "]

class HookHandler(BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_POST(self):
        length = int(self.headers["Content-Length"])
        payload = json.loads(self.rfile.read(length))
        cmd = payload.get("tool_input", {}).get("command", "")

        decision = "allow"
        reason = ""
        for prefix in BLOCKED:
            if cmd.strip().startswith(prefix):
                decision = "deny"
                reason = f"{prefix.strip()} is not allowed outside CI"
                break

        body = json.dumps({
            "hookSpecificOutput": {
                "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
                "permissionDecision": decision,
                "permissionDecisionReason": reason,
            }
        }).encode()
        self.send_response(200)
        self.send_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
        self.end_headers()
        self.wfile.write(body)

    def log_message(self, *_): pass  # suppress access log

HTTPServer(("localhost", 8080), HookHandler).serve_forever()

Prompt hooks send the event context to a Claude model for a natural-language yes/no decision. They are useful when the allow/deny logic is too complex to express in shell but does not justify spawning a full agent:
Agent hooks (experimental) spawn a subagent that can use tools like

to verify conditions before returning a decision. This enables multi-step checks such as: is the file being overwritten tracked in git? Is the dependency being added on the approved-packages list? Does the target path fall inside the project root?
Claude Code injects a set of environment variables into command hook processes, allowing scripts to use project-aware paths without hard-coding them:
Absolute path to the project root (where .claude/ lives)
Space-separated list of files involved in a PostToolUse Edit or Write event
The same session_id as in the JSON payload, useful for log correlation
in hook command strings and inside hook scripts is the idiomatic way to reference project-relative paths, because Claude Code may be invoked from any working directory.
Auto-format TypeScript Files After Every Write
runs the formatter in the background without blocking Claude's next action. The session continues immediately while the format runs.
Inject Git Context into Every Prompt

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# inject-git-context.sh — registered on UserPromptSubmit
set -euo pipefail
cat >/dev/null  # drain stdin (not needed for this hook)

branch=$(git -C "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo 'unknown')
changed=$(git -C "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" diff --name-only HEAD 2>/dev/null | head -10 | tr '\n' ' ')
status=$(git -C "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" status --short 2>/dev/null | head -5)

printf '%s\n' "{\"hookSpecificOutput\":{\"hookEventName\":\"UserPromptSubmit\",\"additionalContext\":\"Git branch: $branch\\nModified files: $changed\\nGit status:\\n$status\"}}"
exit 0

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# run-tests.sh — used with asyncRewake: true on PostToolUse (Edit|Write)
set -euo pipefail
cat >/dev/null  # drain stdin

cd "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR"
if ! npm test --silent 2>&1 | tail -20 > /tmp/test-out.txt; then
  # Exit 2 with failure details on stderr triggers Claude to wake and investigate
  cat /tmp/test-out.txt >&2
  exit 2
fi
exit 0
A team running Claude Code in a regulated financial services environment uses three hooks together to achieve compliance without slowing down development. The first is a
command hook that checks every Bash call against a list of restricted operations (writes outside
, network calls to non-approved hosts, any use of credential-management CLIs). When a blocked pattern is detected it exits 2 with a reason string that tells Claude which policy was violated.
async hook (no asyncRewake) that streams every tool invocation to a structured JSON log via
piped to a log aggregator. Because it is async and only logs, it adds zero latency to the session.
hook that calls the internal JIRA API, finds any ticket ID mentioned in the prompt, and injects the ticket's description and acceptance criteria as

. This means Claude always sees the current ticket requirements without the developer having to paste them. Together, the three hooks add policy enforcement, audit trail, and context enrichment without any changes to how developers interact with Claude.
Calling jq or another command before reading stdin
Stdin is consumed; jq receives empty input and returns null for all fields
Writing debug output to stdout before the decision JSON
Claude Code cannot parse the response; the decision is ignored
Use stderr for debug output; stdout is reserved for the JSON response
Relying on the if filter as a security control
Filter fails open for unparseable commands; attacker can use heredoc or variable expansion to bypass
Apply actual pattern matching inside the hook script, not only in the if field
Doing a slow network call in a UserPromptSubmit hook
Blocks the UI for the duration; default timeout is 30 s but user feels every millisecond
Cache aggressively or use async mode with a local sidecar that pre-fetches context
Claude Code ignores the output; the decision is never applied
Always include hookEventName matching the event being handled
as the first line of every command hook, even if the hook does not inspect the payload. A hook that does not drain stdin may cause a broken-pipe error that kills the session.

: all debug output, warnings, and logs go to stderr. Claude Code displays stderr to the user when a hook exits with code 2 (asyncRewake) or 1 (soft error), making it the right channel for human-readable failure messages.
: for PreToolUse and UserPromptSubmit, target sub-100 ms execution. Avoid network calls in the critical path. Cache external state in a local file and refresh it asynchronously.
: any hook whose only purpose is observation (logging, metrics, audit trail) should set
so it does not block the session regardless of its execution time.
: before deploying, pipe representative JSON payloads into the script and verify both the exit code (via
: do not embed API keys, tokens, or credentials in hook scripts committed to a repo. Reference them as
and inject them through the environment.
: the default command timeout is 600 s — far longer than needed for most hooks. Set a tight
in the hook configuration to prevent a stalled hook from locking the session indefinitely.
Hook latency compounds when multiple hooks are registered on the same event. Claude Code runs handlers within a group
and waits for all to complete before proceeding. If three PreToolUse hooks each take 50 ms, every tool call adds 150 ms of latency. Design hooks to be fast, and prefer
for any hook that does not need to influence the current decision.
For shell scripts, avoid spawning subprocesses (like

) for simple string matching where built-in bash pattern matching suffices — each subprocess fork costs 2–10 ms on a cold kernel. For hooks that run on every tool call (PreToolUse with a broad matcher), this adds up.

HTTP hooks have a minimum latency floor set by the local network roundtrip. For a localhost server this is sub-millisecond; for a remote service it can be 50–500 ms or more. Only use remote HTTP hooks for events where latency is acceptable (PostToolUse, Stop, async events).
: the standard tool for parsing and transforming the JSON payload inside bash hooks. Available on macOS via Homebrew and on Linux via apt/dnf.
: for hooks requiring complex conditional logic, string manipulation, or external API calls. Using
avoids a subprocess fork when invoked from bash.
: suitable for hooks that need npm packages (e.g., YAML parsing, JWT validation). Write the hook as a standalone script with
: for HTTP hooks, any framework that can serve POST requests and return JSON. Use a process manager (systemd, pm2) to keep the server running alongside Claude Code sessions.
Content-aware policy, automation, integration, context injection
Allowing/denying tools by name; fast, no overhead
Guiding Claude's behavior and style; cannot enforce
Exposing capabilities Claude can choose to call; not for enforcement

Hooks run with the permissions of the OS user who started Claude Code — the same user who owns the project files, home directory, and SSH keys. A hook that accepts a URL from the event payload and follows it, or that uses tool input as a shell argument without sanitization, creates a command injection or SSRF vulnerability with full user-level access.
Treat all data from the event payload as untrusted input. Apply the same sanitization you would to user-supplied web input:
when passing payload fields into shell commands to prevent injection via special characters.
— inspect it with pattern matching or a parser, do not execute it.
Validate file paths extracted from the payload against an allowlist of project-relative directories before performing any file operations.
guard on any subprocess the hook spawns to prevent accidental DoS:

Reliability: hooks that exit with code 1 (soft error) allow Claude to continue, which means a bug in a blocking hook silently loses its enforcement effect. Test hooks against adversarial payloads — empty fields, very long strings, Unicode, and newlines embedded in values — before relying on them for policy enforcement.
must be the first command in any bash command hook — reading stdin twice causes the second read to see nothing.
: stdout carries the JSON decision that Claude Code acts on; stderr carries human-readable diagnostics that surface in the session log and asyncRewake messages.
: for PreToolUse hooks it blocks the tool call; for asyncRewake hooks it wakes Claude with the hook's stderr as context. exit 1 is a soft failure that logs and continues.
: the inner object under hookSpecificOutput must include hookEventName matching the event being handled — omitting it causes Claude Code to silently discard the response.
: command (most flexible, runs any executable), http (integrates external services), prompt (natural-language decisions via a Claude model), agent (multi-step verification using Read/Grep/Glob).
: hooks run with full user permissions, so failing to sanitize fields before using them in shell commands can lead to command injection with the user's own access level.
: run tests or validators in the background with asyncRewake, exit 2 on failure, and Claude wakes automatically to investigate — without any manual monitoring.
: pipe mock payloads into the hook script from the command line, verify exit codes and stdout JSON, and test adversarial inputs before registering the hook in a live session.
